text:
This issue of New World Solidarity deals with two apparently distant topics. In the first article, it is discussed how hydroponics can become a new method of growing plants through solutions that have a higher concentration of minerals than in soil, thus increasing the productivity of the sector. At the same time, globally the World Federation of Trade Unions (WFTU) is undergoing a tremendous revival and it has also made a great stride in South Africa. Four affiliates of the Congress of South African Trade Unions (COSATU) – the largest confederation in the ITUC – have already affiliated in the WFTU, triggering a heated debate within the ranks of labour internationalism regarding the prospects for a united labor movement worldwide.

In February 2012 the Presidential Council of the WFTU (World Federation of Trade Unions) gathered in Johannesburg, South Africa. During the meeting the President of COSATU (Congress of South African Trade Unions), Sdumo Dlamini, declared his willingness to reassess the unions in the international framework mentioning a possible bridge between the ITUC and the WFTU. During the 11th National COSATU Congress, the General Secretary of the ITUC, Sharan Burrow, and the General Secretary of the WFTU, George Mavrikos, made important speeches, as the union leaders are trying to manage the fragile balance between the two organisations. However, it seems that the break is irreversible. Burrow spoke about the “pluralism and the freedom of choice” avoiding to enter into ideological debates about socialism or communism, and admitting a “fundamental difference” with the WFTU regarding the way each organisation deals with the events of the Arab Spring and with the current wave of revolution in Syria and other countries worldwide. The question remains, whether the recent developments are a symptom of a split in the international trade union movement, which appears to be irreversible, or an opportunity for the rebirth of the labour movement, in a pluralistic and more dynamic way.
